ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Sup Fucuk (Malay beancurd skin clear soup). Mapped to closest parent 'soup food product' (FOODON_03315595) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Malay beancurd skin soup. A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay soup preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sup Fucuk is approximately one bowl of 300 ml (300 g), which is a typical home-cooked portion served as a side dish alongside rice at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database — Soy-based and vegetable soup entries
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research, Malaysia)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sup Fucuk is a traditional Malay home-cooked soup. Nutrient values are adapted from the Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) using the closest available entries for beancurd skin (fucuk), glass noodles (suun), and vegetable broth components. Values represent a composite estimate for the prepared dish at approximately 100 g serving weight. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and mung bean noodles were used as cross-reference to validate protein, fat, and carbohydrate ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — bean curd / tofu and glass noodle analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2021), Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sup Fucuk. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy: dried beancurd skin (fucuk) has a GI of approximately 30–40 (tofu/soy protein foods, University of Sydney GI Database); mung bean glass noodles (suun) have a reported GI of approximately 39 (Atkinson et al. 2021). The vegetable broth and fibre content further moderate glycemic response. A composite GI of 35 (low) is defensible. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.3 g (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 180 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 540 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit added salt and high-sodium seasonings when preparing Sup Fucuk, as a full bowl provides approximately 540 mg sodium (23% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), which is moderate and manageable within a low-sodium meal plan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) driven by soy protein in fucuk and fibre from vegetables; moderate carbohydrate load of 5 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sup Fucuk is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index of approximately 35 and low glycemic load per serving (~5.3), making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation when consumed as a side dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.3 g per 100 g), soy isoflavones from fucuk, and potassium from vegetables support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sup Fucuk is a heart-friendly side dish with low saturated fat content and soy-derived protein from fucuk, which may contribute to modest LDL cholesterol reduction; however, sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones in fucuk have mild anti-inflammatory properties; vegetables provide antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sup Fucuk may be consumed freely by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the soy-based fucuk and vegetable components provide anti-inflammatory phytonutrients and antioxidants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Fucuk is a traditional Malay clear soup made primarily from fucuk (dried beancurd skin) and suun (glass noodles made from mung bean starch), simmered in a light aromatic broth flavoured with garlic, shallots, and ginger. The soup often includes vegetables such as carrot and cabbage, and may optionally contain small pieces of chicken or prawns. The dish is a common home-cooked side dish in Malaysia and Singapore, valued for its delicate, silky texture from the rehydrated beancurd skin, the translucent chewiness of the glass noodles, and the clean, savoury flavour of the broth. Nutritionally, Sup Fucuk is low in calories (approximately 45 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate plant-based protein (3.2 g per 100 g) from the soy-derived fucuk, and has a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking a light, nutritious meal. The sodium content of approximately 180 mg per 100 g warrants attention for those on sodium-restricted diets. The dish is naturally halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and can be prepared as a vegetarian dish by omitting meat or seafood. It is typically served at lunch or dinner as a side dish alongside steamed rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sup Fucuk?
Is Sup Fucuk suitable for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in Sup Fucuk?
What is the glycemic index of Sup Fucuk?

confidence_reason: Sup Fucuk as a named dish does not appear as a discrete entry in the Malaysian MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from MyFCD component entries for beancurd skin, glass noodles, and vegetable broth, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for tofu skin and mung bean noodles. The GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for soy protein foods and mung bean glass noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this is a plant-based soup with no animal-derived ingredients in the base recipe; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods. If chicken or prawns are added, trace B12 would be present. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in this plant-based broth preparation with no fortified ingredients. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: the base recipe is plant-based (fucuk is soy-derived); confirmed negligible in the absence of animal ingredients. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. 5. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the prepared soup as a composite dish is not reported in MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae: the input reported 120 mcg as retinol activity equivalents; this has been revised to 20 mcg RAE as a more conservative and defensible estimate for a vegetable-broth soup without liver or high-beta-carotene ingredients — the original value of 120 mcg RAE appears inflated for this dish type. 7. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for tofu skin and mung bean noodles proportionally weighted to the dish composition. 8. The dish is tagged vegetarian in the base recipe; if chicken or prawns are added, the vegetarian flag should be set to false. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (15 g for 300 g portion) / 100 = 5.3 g (low GL). 10. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact match exists for this traditional Malay soup preparation.
